context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. | Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Informal Economy and Illegality

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Most poor people in developing countries – which means the majority – live in informal settlements which are also often illegal in many ways. How does informalization happen? What is the relationship between the informal and formal economy? Who plans for outcomes in informal economies? Is formalization — of tenure arrangements etc. — the answer?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection on informal economy + illegality, and ngl I’m kinda stuck on how to frame it. Can u help me break down the prompt into the main questions I should answer?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's get started! Before I weigh in, why don't you read the prompt carefully one more time and tell me: what are the distinct questions it's actually asking you? List what you see.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and asking for help breaking down the prompt. This is a reasonable first step. Rather than listing the questions for them, I should use the Socratic method to have them identify the key questions themselves. This keeps the student active in the process and avoids doing the work for them. I'll ask them to read the prompt carefully and tell me what they see.

exchanges.student: ok, I think it’s asking: how informalization happens, how informal and formal economies connect, who actually does the planning, and whether formalization is the fix? I’m not fully sure if I’m missing a bigger theme tho.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you've got the four questions right. Now, what do you think connects all four of them? Is there a central tension or idea that runs through all of them together?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the four sub-questions in the prompt. Now I should affirm this briefly and push them to think about the bigger connective theme themselves, using a guiding question rather than revealing it. This keeps the Socratic method in play and moves toward the substance of the assignment.

exchanges.student: maybe the big idea is that “informal” isn’t separate from the state/formal system, it kinda gets produced by it too? idk if the tension is legality vs survival, or planning from above vs what people do on the ground.
exchanges.tutor: Both of those tensions are sharp observations. Now, do you think they contradict each other, or could one actually be a consequence of the other? Think about that before deciding on your framing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing genuine insight here - they've identified two strong tensions. I should affirm the quality of their thinking without giving away which framing is best, and push them to commit to one or develop the relationship between the two ideas they raised. This moves the conversation productively toward their actual writing.

exchanges.student: I think they connect tbh—if top-down systems exclude people, then ppl survive through “illegal” or informal ways. So legality vs survival might come out of planning/power, not be separate from it.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong framing - hold onto it, it could anchor your whole piece. So let's dig into the first question: how does informalization actually happen? What forces or conditions do you think drive people into informal settlements and economies?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a strong connective insight linking power, exclusion, and informality. This is good conceptual progress. I should affirm this briefly and now push them toward the first substantive question - how informalization actually happens - to start building the body of their reflection. I want to keep momentum going toward actual content.

exchanges.student: umm rapid urbanization, not enough affordable housing/jobs, weak state capacity maybe, and laws ppl can’t realistically meet. Also maybe land markets and bureaucracy push low-income ppl out?
exchanges.tutor: Good list. You mentioned laws people can't realistically meet - can you say more about that? Who sets those laws, and whose interests do they reflect?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has generated a solid list of drivers. I should affirm briefly and push them to go deeper on one of these rather than staying at the list level, which will help them develop analytical depth for the reflection. I'll focus on the laws/exclusion point since it connects well to their earlier framing about power.

exchanges.student: I guess the state sets them, but prob also elites shape them a lot. Like zoning, land titling, permits, labor rules can favor ppl already inside the formal system, so others end up “illegal” by default.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly - informality isn't just an accident, it can be structurally produced. Now, given that, how do you think the informal and formal economies actually relate to each other? Are they separate worlds, or something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building a strong argument connecting state power, elite interests, and the production of illegality. This connects well to the course themes of planning, power, and legitimacy. Now I should push them toward the second question - the relationship between formal and informal economies - since they've got good momentum and this is a natural next step.

exchanges.student: they def don’t seem separate lol. informal workers/services often support formal firms and cities, and formal actors can profit from informality while acting like it’s outside the system.
exchanges.tutor: Good - so formality and informality are intertwined, not opposed. That raises an interesting question for your reflection: if the state isn't fully present in informal settlements, who actually shapes outcomes there? Who does the 'planning'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a key insight about the symbiotic and exploitative relationship between formal and informal economies. This is solid progress. Now I should push them toward the third question - who plans in informal economies - since this is where the course's theme of planning, power, and non-state actors becomes really relevant.

exchanges.student: maybe local brokers, landlords, NGOs, community leaders, gangs sometimes, and residents themselves through daily negotiation? I’m a bit unsure how to say that without sounding like “anything people do = planning.”
exchanges.tutor: That's a really sharp concern. So what do you think distinguishes planning from just... surviving? What would need to be true for what these actors do to count as planning?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a range of non-state planning actors and is showing good critical self-awareness about the conceptual boundaries of 'planning.' This is exactly the kind of nuance worth developing. I should validate their concern and help them think through what distinguishes planning from just survival behavior, without giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: maybe planning means some intentional shaping of space/rules/resources over time, not just one-off coping. I’m still fuzzy tho—can I frame it as fragmented or informal governance instead of calling all of it planning?
exchanges.tutor: That framing works well and shows real analytical care. Now let's move to the last question, which is probably the most contested: is formalization - like land titling or tenure regularization - actually the answer to informality? What's your instinct?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a nuanced and defensible conceptual distinction and is proposing a useful framing. I should affirm their reasoning briefly and push them toward the final question - formalization as a solution - since we're nearing the end of the planned conversation length and they need to cover all parts of the prompt.

exchanges.student: my instinct is “not automatically” bc it can give security, but it can also raise costs, trigger eviction/market pressure, or ignore why ppl were informal in the first place. I think I wanna argue partial formalization can help if it protects ppl instead of just disciplining them.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong and defensible argument. You now have all the pieces: a central framing, answers to all four questions, and a clear position on formalization. Rather than keep discussing, I'd encourage you to start drafting. What would your opening sentence or argument look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has developed a nuanced, well-reasoned position on formalization that captures both its potential benefits and risks. This is excellent progress. We've now covered all four sub-questions and they have a clear argument emerging. Since we've been building up a lot of conceptual ground across several exchanges, and given the redundancy rule, this is a good moment to push the student to integrate everything into a written attempt rather than continuing to discuss.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. Every key idea was drawn from the student through targeted questions rather than being stated by the tutor first. The tutor built progressively on each student response, maintained concise and supportive turns, stayed anchored to the assignment, and avoided any near-submission-ready output. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave the student a conclusion, final framing, or submission-ready content. All four sub-questions were explored through student-generated answers, with the tutor only affirming and probing further. 1.2: Each turn built directly on the student's prior response, and the tutor diagnosed confusion (e.g., Turn 8 on what counts as planning) before redirecting. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback, notably in Turn 9 affirming 'fragmented governance' as analytically careful framing. 2.1: The dialogue advanced steadily through all four prompt questions without cycling or repetition. 2.2: The conversation remained tightly anchored to the assignment throughout. 3.1: Tutor responses were consistently brief and interactive. 3.2: Tone was warm, encouraging, and formative throughout, with no grading language.
